evidence_quotes.quote: In-game currencies exist solely within bounded game systems.
Displaying euro equivalents for earned rewards misleads players into believing their gameplay achievements have real-world monetary value, which they clearly do not.

paragraphs.text: Issue: Price Display Requirements May Misrepresent the Value of Earned vs. Purchased Currency The Guidelines require displaying real-world monetary equivalents alongside in-game currency prices to ensure transparency. The guidelines create unintended confusion in hybrid currency systems where players can both purchase and earn the same currency through gameplay. Design Challenge: - Players can purchase 100 gems for 0.99 - Players can also earn 100 gems by completing a boss fight - A Sword in-game costs 100 gems Under my current interpretation: When displaying the item price as "100 gems (0.99)," this creates confusion: It applies real-world value for all use cases: - Purchased gems: The display accurately reflects real-world cost - Earned gems: The display falsely implies the player earned 0.99 in real monetary value What's the Problem? In-game currencies exist solely within bounded game systems. They cannot be withdrawn or converted to real money. Displaying euro equivalents for earned rewards misleads players into believing their gameplay achievements have real-world monetary value, which they clearly do not. Recommendation: For in-game currencies that can be both earned through gameplay and purchased with real money, euro displays should apply only at the point of purchase. Goal: Be transparent about real-money purchases without making earned rewards seem to have real-world value.
